ideas.Duration (durée) vs. spatialized time:
**Duration** (*la durée*) is Henri Bergson's central and most influential concept — his account of **real, lived time** as we actually experience it in consciousness, which he sharply distinguishes from the **spatialized, mathematical time** measured by clocks and used by science. This distinction is the foundation of Bergson's entire philosophy and one of the most important contributions to modern thought about time, consciousness, and life.

Bergson argued that we habitually misunderstand time by **spatializing** it — representing it as a homogeneous medium, a line composed of discrete, separate, identical instants laid out side by side like points in space, and measured by counting units (seconds, minutes). This is the "time" of physics and everyday practical life: divisible, quantitative, reversible in conception, and treated as a kind of fourth dimension of space. But this, Bergson insists, is not *real* time at all; it is a distortion, a spatial symbol substituted for the genuine thing.

**Real time — duration —** as we live it in our inner conscious experience, is utterly different. It is a **continuous, indivisible, flowing** stream in which past, present, and future melt into one another and interpenetrate; it is **qualitative**, not quantitative — a ceaseless becoming in which each moment is unique and colored by the whole of what has gone before, so that nothing exactly repeats. Duration is heterogeneous, ever-novel, and creative; it cannot be broken into separate units without being falsified, any more than a melody can be understood as a mere sequence of isolated notes. This intuition of real, lived duration — irreducible to the spatial, measurable time of science — is the key to Bergson's accounts of consciousness, freedom, memory, and life, and it profoundly influenced modern literature (the "stream of consciousness"), philosophy, and thought about time.
ideas.The élan vital:
The ***élan vital*** — the "**vital impulse**" or "vital force" — is one of Henri Bergson's most famous and influential ideas, the central concept of his major work *Creative Evolution* (1907). It names the creative, striving, living energy that Bergson held to be the driving force of **life** and its **evolution** — a dynamic impulse pushing life ever onward toward greater complexity, novelty, and freedom, and it represents his powerful alternative to the mechanistic biology of his day.

Bergson developed the *élan vital* in response to what he saw as the failure of the two dominant scientific accounts of **evolution** — mechanistic **Darwinism** (evolution by chance variation and natural selection) and teleological **finalism** (evolution directed toward a pre-set goal). Both, Bergson argued, misconceive life by treating it in terms borrowed from inert matter and spatialized time: mechanism reduces life to the reassembly of pre-existing parts by external causes, while finalism reduces it to the realization of a fixed, predetermined plan. Neither can account for the genuine **creativity, novelty, and unpredictability** of life's evolution.

Against them, Bergson posited the *élan vital*: a single, original impulse of life, surging up through matter, which drives evolution not by mechanical push nor toward a fixed end, but through a genuinely **creative, unforeseeable** unfolding — continually generating new forms, diverging along many branches (like the divergence of plants, insects, and vertebrates), and overcoming the resistance of inert matter. Life is thus an ongoing creative process, akin to the flow of **duration**, in which the future is truly open and new. Though the *élan vital* was later criticized by scientists and philosophers as vague or unscientific vitalism, it captured a genuine sense of life's creativity and dynamism, and it was enormously influential — inspiring process philosophy, vitalist and organicist currents, and thinkers from Whitehead to Deleuze.
ideas.Intuition as method:
**Intuition** is Henri Bergson's proposed philosophical **method** — the special mode of knowing by which, he argued, we can grasp the true, inner reality of things (above all, **duration** and life), in contrast to the **intellect** (or "analysis"), which is suited only to the practical manipulation of matter and inevitably distorts living reality. The distinction between intuition and intellect is central to Bergson's epistemology and to his critique of conventional science and philosophy.

Bergson argued that the human **intellect** evolved primarily as a practical instrument — a tool for *acting on* the material world, for fabricating and using inert solids. As such, it is superbly adapted to analyzing matter: it works by breaking things down into discrete, fixed, separate parts, by spatializing, quantifying, and representing reality through static concepts and symbols. This is precisely what science does, and it is immensely useful for control and prediction. But this analytic, spatializing intellect is fundamentally **unsuited to grasping life, consciousness, movement, and time as they really are** — for these are continuous, flowing, indivisible, qualitative, and ever-novel (they are *duration*), and the intellect can only falsify them by chopping them into static fragments, as it turns the living flow into a series of frozen snapshots.

To grasp the inner reality of duration, life, and consciousness, Bergson held, we need a different faculty: **intuition** — a kind of direct, sympathetic, immediate insight by which we place ourselves *within* the object, coinciding with its unique, inner, flowing reality rather than viewing it from outside through concepts. Intuition is a sympathetic entering-into the very movement and duration of a thing, an absolute rather than relative knowledge. Where the intellect goes *around* its object with symbols, intuition enters *into* it. Bergson's exaltation of intuition over analysis as the road to metaphysical truth was central to his influence and to his challenge to the pretensions of a purely scientific, intellectualist understanding of reality.
ideas.Memory and matter:
The relationship between **memory** and **matter** — and, through it, between **mind and body** — is the central concern of Henri Bergson's important work *Matter and Memory* (1896), in which he develops a subtle and original theory of the mind, perception, and the mind-body relation that seeks to avoid both crude materialism and pure idealism. His analysis of **memory** in particular is one of his most influential achievements.

Bergson used the phenomenon of memory to argue against the prevailing view that mental states are simply identical with, or wholly produced by, states of the **brain** and matter. He distinguished two fundamentally different kinds of memory. **Habit-memory** is the bodily, mechanical memory of learned actions and skills (like reciting a lesson by rote or riding a bicycle) — memory that is stored in and enacted through the body's motor mechanisms, oriented toward present action. **Pure memory** (or "recollection"), by contrast, is the memory of unique past *events* — the spontaneous, dated recollection of particular moments of our lives, which Bergson argued is **not** stored in the brain as a material trace at all, but survives in a purely spiritual form, in the past itself, which endures. The whole of our past, he held, is preserved in this pure memory and presses upon the present.

The **brain**, on Bergson's account, is not a storehouse of memories but an organ of **action and selection** — a kind of "telephone exchange" that filters and channels perception and memory toward useful bodily response, admitting into consciousness only those memories relevant to present action. Perception itself is oriented not toward pure knowledge but toward possible action on things. This intricate theory allowed Bergson to argue for the **irreducibility of mind (spirit) to matter** — to defend the reality and independence of consciousness and the spiritual past against reductive materialism — while still giving the body and brain their genuine (if limited) role. Rich and influential, his analysis of memory, time, and the mind-body relation shaped psychology, philosophy, and later thinkers such as Deleuze.

works.Time and Free Will:
*Time and Free Will* (*Essai sur les données immédiates de la conscience*, "Essay on the Immediate Data of Consciousness," 1889) is Henri Bergson's first major work — originally his doctoral dissertation — and the book in which he introduced his foundational concept of **duration** (*la durée*) and mounted his influential defense of human **freedom**. It laid the groundwork for his entire philosophy and established the central themes he would develop throughout his career.

The book's central achievement is the distinction between two conceptions of **time**. Bergson argues that we habitually misrepresent time by **spatializing** it — treating it as a homogeneous, quantitative, divisible medium, like a line made of separate instants, as science and everyday practical life require. But this spatialized time is a falsification. **Real time**, as immediately given in consciousness, is **duration** — a continuous, indivisible, qualitative, ever-flowing stream in which moments interpenetrate and nothing exactly repeats. Our inner conscious life is not a series of separate states laid side by side but a dynamic, flowing whole.

From this analysis Bergson builds his defense of **free will**. The classic debate between determinism and free will, he argues, rests on a fundamental error: it conceives of psychological states and choices in **spatialized** terms — as separate, measurable things arranged like points, so that a decision appears as the mechanical outcome of pre-existing causes (determinism) or as an arbitrary choice between fixed alternatives. But once we understand the self and its states as **duration** — as a continuous, dynamic, interpenetrating flow — the whole problem is transformed. Genuine free acts, Bergson argues, are those that spring from the whole of our flowing, evolving personality, expressing our deep self in its continuous development; they cannot be predicted or reduced to mechanical causation because they are creative and novel. Freedom is real, but it is misconceived when we spatialize the self. Original and influential, *Time and Free Will* introduced Bergson's revolutionary ideas about time, consciousness, and freedom.
works.Matter and Memory:
*Matter and Memory* (*Matière et mémoire*, 1896) is one of Henri Bergson's most important and philosophically substantial works — a subtle and original investigation into the nature of **perception**, **memory**, and the **relationship between mind and body**, in which he develops a theory designed to overcome the traditional opposition of materialism and idealism. It is widely regarded as his most rigorous philosophical achievement and has been especially influential in recent decades (notably on Gilles Deleuze).

The work's guiding problem is the **mind-body relation**, which Bergson approaches through a careful analysis of **memory**. He argues against the then-dominant view that memories are simply stored as material traces in the **brain** and that mind is reducible to brain states. Central to his argument is a distinction between two kinds of memory: **habit-memory**, the bodily, mechanical memory of learned skills and actions, which is indeed rooted in the body's motor mechanisms; and **pure memory** or recollection, the spontaneous remembering of unique past events, which Bergson argues is **not** material at all and is not stored in the brain, but subsists in a spiritual form — for the entire past, he holds, endures and is preserved in itself.

On this basis Bergson reconceives the role of the **brain and perception**. The brain is not a container of images or memories but an organ of **action**: it selects, from the vast field of memory and perception, only what is useful for the body's present response to its environment. Perception itself is oriented toward possible **action** on things, not toward disinterested contemplation. Through this intricate account, Bergson defends the **irreducibility of mind and memory to matter** — securing the reality and independence of consciousness and the enduring past against reductive materialism — while still granting the body and brain a genuine role in orienting us toward action. Deep and challenging, *Matter and Memory* is a landmark in the philosophy of mind and memory.
works.Creative Evolution:
*Creative Evolution* (*L'Évolution créatrice*, 1907) is Henri Bergson's most famous and popular work — the book that brought him international fame, made him one of the most celebrated philosophers of his age, and contributed to his winning the Nobel Prize in Literature (1927). In it Bergson applies his philosophy of **duration** to the phenomenon of **life** and biological **evolution**, developing his celebrated concept of the ***élan vital*** (the "vital impulse") and mounting a powerful critique of mechanistic science.

The work confronts the great question of **evolution**, and Bergson argues that the reigning scientific accounts — mechanistic **Darwinism** (chance variation and natural selection) and **finalism** (evolution toward a predetermined goal) — both fundamentally misunderstand life. Both, he contends, illegitimately apply to living reality the categories proper to inert **matter** and to spatialized, mathematical time: mechanism treats life as the mere rearrangement of pre-existing parts, finalism as the unfolding of a fixed plan. Neither can grasp the genuine **creativity, novelty, and unpredictability** that Bergson sees as the essence of life's evolution.

In their place, Bergson offers his vision of the ***élan vital***: a single, original impulse of life that surges up through matter, driving evolution as a genuinely **creative**, open-ended, unforeseeable process — continually inventing new forms, diverging along multiple paths, and overcoming the inertia and resistance of matter, much as the flow of duration continually generates novelty. Life is thus a creative movement, not a mechanism; the future is truly open. The book also contains Bergson's important reflections on the **intellect** (evolved to handle matter and hence prone to "spatialize" and mechanize) versus **intuition** (which alone can grasp the inner movement of life), and a critique of the intellect's tendency to reconstitute movement out of static snapshots (the "cinematographic" illusion). Eloquent, imaginative, and enormously influential, *Creative Evolution* shaped process philosophy, vitalism, modernist literature and art, and thinkers from Whitehead to Deleuze.
